question: The fundamental tension in international marketing strategy is between:
options: Choosing between advertising and public relations in foreign markets

Standardization (using the same product, pricing, positioning, and communication globally) vs. adaptation (modifying the marketing mix for each local market); standardization benefits: economies of scale (one product, one marketing campaign, global), brand consistency (same message globally), efficiency (one R&D investment, one supply chain), and coordination simplicity; adaptation benefits: better local fit (product meets local needs), competitive relevance (local products/competitors addressed), legal compliance (local regulations), and cultural resonance (locally relevant communication); the classic debate: Theodore Levitt (Harvard, 1983) argued globalization of markets means standardization is optimal ('customers will accept quality goods at lower prices') vs. Philip Kotler and others who argued local differences remain significant and adaptation is required; the modern view: 'glocalization' — standardize the elements that are truly universal (core brand values, product quality, key technology platform) and adapt the elements that must be local (flavors, sizes, communication, distribution)
Whether to use social media or traditional advertising globally
Deciding between domestic and export pricing strategies
explanation: Standardization vs. adaptation in practice: McDonald's is the most studied example. The standardized elements: golden arches, quality/service/cleanliness/value promise, process operations system (how food is made), franchise management system. The adapted elements: menu (McAloo Tikki in India, Teriyaki burger in Japan, McLobster in Maine, beer in Germany), store design (some markets have table service, not just counter service), ingredients (halal meat in Muslim-majority markets, no beef in India). The financial logic of standardization: developing a new product globally costs the same R&D investment; if the product is identical, each additional market is incremental profit with minimal additional cost. The financial logic of adaptation: a product that fits local preferences will capture more revenue than a globally standardized product that partially fits — if the adaptation cost is less than the revenue uplift, adapt.

question: Coca-Cola's marketing strategy in China uses different advertising themes than in the US. In the US, Coca-Cola advertising focuses on individual happiness, refreshment, and personal enjoyment. In China, Coca-Cola advertising focuses on family gatherings, sharing, and collective celebration. What cultural framework explains this difference?
options: Coca-Cola simply has larger advertising budgets in China that allow for different themes

Hofstede's individualism vs. collectivism dimension drives communication adaptation: US (IDV = 91 — highly individualistic): advertising resonates when it connects the product to personal benefit, individual experience, and self-expression ('Taste the feeling' — individual refreshment moment); Chinese consumers (IDV = 20 — highly collectivist): advertising resonates when it connects the product to group benefit, social harmony, and family/group celebration; a Chinese consumer's decision to purchase Coca-Cola is influenced by 'how will this contribute to the group event?' not 'how will this make me feel individually?'; examples: US Coke ad: young person discovers Coke during personal journey; Chinese Coke ad: family reunion dinner, grandmother and grandchildren sharing Coke together; the marketing implication: using US-developed advertising content in China will be less effective not because it's bad advertising, but because the emotional resonance doesn't transfer — it's speaking to an individual who doesn't exist in the same way in Chinese culture
Coca-Cola uses different advertising to test which approach has higher recall rates
Different advertising in different countries is purely a regulatory requirement
explanation: Cultural values in advertising: academic research consistently shows that culturally-consistent advertising (advertising whose emotional themes match the cultural values of the target audience) outperforms culturally-inconsistent advertising in recall, persuasion, and purchase intent. The individualism-collectivism dimension has the clearest documented effect on advertising effectiveness. The LG Electronics adaptation: LG's Korean origin (collectivist culture) means its product development tends toward family-sharing features; when marketing in the US, LG must translate these to individual benefit frames. The Nike China adaptation: Nike's 'Just Do It' individualist achievement frame resonates strongly in the US; in China, Nike adapted its campaigns to include community achievement ('We are all athletes') themes — a collectivist frame applied to an individualist concept. The limits of adaptation: Coca-Cola maintains global brand consistency (the logo, the signature color, the product) while adapting communication themes — this is the glocalization balance. Changing the fundamental brand positioning (what Coca-Cola represents as a brand) by market would create confusion for internationally mobile consumers and destroy brand equity built globally.

question: Apple sells the same iPhone globally at approximately the same price point (adjusted for local purchasing power parity). McDonald's sells Big Macs at radically different prices globally ($5.69 in the US vs. $2.10 in India vs. $6.80 in Switzerland). What product and market characteristics explain these different pricing approaches?
options: Apple and McDonald's use identical pricing strategies globally

Product type, production economics, and local competition drive different optimal pricing approaches: Apple's global pricing consistency: (1) The iPhone is a globally manufactured product with global supply chain — cost structure is the same regardless of sale country; (2) The product is a status item with globally understood positioning; premium pricing is part of the product proposition in every market; (3) Grey market risk: if Apple priced iPhones significantly cheaper in India than the US, arbitrageurs would buy iPhones in India and sell them in the US — Apple must maintain roughly comparable prices (adjusted for taxes and import duties) to prevent grey market; (4) Apple's premium brand positioning is global — cutting price in one market signals that the market is 'second tier,' damaging the brand. McDonald's local pricing: (1) McDonald's raw materials and labor are sourced locally — the cost structure in India is radically different from Switzerland; (2) McDonald's competes against local Indian restaurants charging 20-50 rupees ($0.25-0.60) per meal; pricing at US levels would make McDonald's completely inaccessible to the Indian middle class; (3) No grey market risk: you can't buy a McAloo Tikki in India and sell it in Switzerland; the perishable, local-consumption product has no arbitrage risk
Pricing should always be identical globally to prevent customer confusion
Local competition has no effect on international pricing strategy
explanation: Pricing strategy dimensions for international markets: (1) Grey market risk — high for standardized products (electronics, luxury goods, pharmaceuticals) that can be shipped internationally; constrains price variation because arbitrageurs close price gaps; (2) Cost structure variation — companies producing locally can price locally; companies producing centrally face grey market risk; (3) Competitive positioning — what are the competitive alternatives in the local market? (4) Income distribution — what is the distribution of purchasing power in the market? Apple's India strategy: Apple has introduced older iPhone models at lower price points for the Indian market (not just lower prices, but genuinely different products) rather than pricing the iPhone 15 at Indian income levels — maintaining premium positioning while accessing the growing Indian market through product line extension rather than price reduction. The 'Big Mac Index' (The Economist): the famous purchasing power comparison tool uses Big Mac prices globally to compare the relative purchasing power of different currencies — it works precisely because Big Macs are locally produced and locally consumed, making them free from grey market effects.

question: Netflix launched in 130 countries simultaneously in January 2016, offering the same platform, same interface, and mostly the same content globally. Within 3 years, Netflix was investing $1B+ in local content production (Korean dramas, Indian films, Spanish telenovelas). What does this strategic evolution reveal about standardization vs. adaptation in streaming?
options: Netflix's local content investment was a mistake because standardized global content is always optimal

Platform standardization enabled market entry; content adaptation proved necessary for sustained growth: Netflix's initial hypothesis: global content + global platform → global success; the learning: English-language US content resonates globally for some segments (educated, English-proficient, globally mobile consumers) but misses the majority of viewers in non-English markets; Korea: Korean dramas had devoted domestic fan bases; 'Squid Game' (2021) proved Korean content had global appeal, becoming Netflix's most-watched show globally; the local content investment thesis: locally-produced content creates local cultural resonance (Korean viewers prefer Korean stories; Indian viewers prefer Indian context); local content creates competitive differentiation vs. domestic competitors (Indian streaming services Hotstar/JioCinema have better access to Bollywood; Netflix needed original Indian content); local content creates global export potential (Squid Game exported from Korea to 190 countries is more efficient than producing 190 different shows); the glocalization model: global platform (technical infrastructure, recommendation algorithm, UI/UX); global content library (US shows, UK documentaries); local content investment (original content for each major market); the local content creates both local relevance and global discovery value
Netflix should have invested only in local content from the beginning
Streaming services require no content adaptation because content preferences are globally homogeneous
explanation: Netflix's local content ROI: 'Squid Game' cost ~$21M to produce and generated $900M+ in value to Netflix (subscriber acquisition and retention, according to Bloomberg's analysis). This 43:1 ROI on a Korean-language show illustrates that local content investment can generate global returns when the content quality is high and the subject matter transcends local context. The French 'Lupin' (French-language adaptation of classic French literary character) attracted 70M+ households in its first month — the majority outside France. The strategic insight: 'local' content is actually 'authentically specific' content; audiences globally prefer authentic specific stories over generic global content. 'Emily in Paris' (US production set in Paris) attracted large audiences but generated cultural controversy in France for stereotyping French culture; 'Lupin' (genuinely French) had far greater French resonance and exported more successfully. The local production investment creates: authentic cultural content, local production industry relationships, regulatory goodwill in markets that require local content quotas (EU content rules require 30% local content on streaming platforms operating in EU countries).

question: Unilever manages 400+ brands across 190 countries. Some brands are global (Dove, Lipton, Axe/Lynx); some are local (specific regional food brands, local cleaning products). Unilever's management of this portfolio has oscillated between centralization (one global brand, one product formula) and localization (local brand management, local formulas). What strategic principle should guide the balance?
options: Unilever should standardize all 400+ brands into a single global brand to maximize efficiency

Brand purpose and competitive advantage determine the optimal degree of standardization for each brand: the Dove example (global standardization works): Dove's 'Real Beauty' positioning (challenging beauty stereotypes) resonates across cultures because beauty insecurity is a universal human experience; using the same campaign globally creates efficiency and builds cumulative brand equity; the campaign translates across cultures because the emotional insight is universal. The local food brand example (localization works): food preferences are deeply culturally specific; a soy sauce brand in Indonesia doesn't translate to a German market; maintaining local brands with local management preserves cultural authenticity that global management would destroy; the competitive advantage question: does the brand's competitive advantage derive from: (a) universal human insight (emotions, values, benefits that translate globally → standardize) or (b) culturally-specific preference, knowledge, or positioning (→ protect local brand management). The efficiency argument: 400 brands × 190 countries = 76,000 brand-market combinations; managing each independently is expensive; Unilever's '5-15-30' brand strategy: focus investment on top 5 global brands, next 15 scaling brands, top 30 country brands; everything else managed for profitability not growth
Unilever should always prioritize local market preferences over global brand efficiency
Consumer goods companies should always use local brands to avoid cultural insensitivity
explanation: Unilever's brand portfolio management: Unilever has 400+ brands (after years of brand rationalization from 1,600+) spanning personal care, food, and home care. The rationalization: CEO Niall FitzGerald (1990s) recognized that managing 1,600 brands was operationally unmanageable — too many product variants, too many local manufacturing setups, too little investment per brand. The 'path to growth' strategy (2000) cut to 400 power brands. The local food brand question: Unilever's food portfolio includes many local brands (Wall's ice cream = global; Kwality Wall's in India; Ola in Netherlands; Langnese in Germany; Kibon in Brazil — all the same product with local names). The decision to maintain local brand names rather than standardizing to 'Wall's' everywhere: research showed that local brand names have higher consumer affinity in some markets; rebranding costs (packaging, advertising) must be weighed against efficiency gains; the trust factor (local brand = 'this is our brand') in markets with historical suspicion of multinational companies.

question: A B2B software company (project management platform) is expanding from the US to Germany and Japan. Their US product: English-language only, priced in USD, free trial → conversion sales model, direct online signup, no local support. The German and Japanese enterprise markets they're targeting have: IT procurement through formal RFP processes, strong preferences for local language, data privacy requirements (GDPR Germany, APPI Japan), and expectations of direct vendor relationships. What standardization-vs-adaptation decisions must the company make?
options: B2B software companies require no adaptation for international markets because software is universal

Enterprise B2B market entry requires structural adaptation across multiple dimensions: the standardization candidates (what can remain the same): core product functionality (project management features); global platform infrastructure; pricing model structure (subscription); security certifications (SOC 2 Type II is globally recognized). The adaptation requirements: (1) Language: enterprise software in Germany requires German interface — German enterprises will not approve software that requires English proficiency; translation is the minimum; localization (German UX conventions, date formats, currency) is required; (2) Data residency (GDPR): German enterprises will require that data is stored on EU servers; US-hosted data violates GDPR for EU personal data; AWS/Azure EU region infrastructure required; possibly separate EU data instance; (3) Sales model: US self-serve model (free trial → online signup → credit card) doesn't work for German and Japanese enterprise; procurement goes through formal IT committee and legal review; companies need local sales representatives who speak German/Japanese and can navigate procurement processes; (4) Japan-specific: Japanese enterprise software procurement requires physical presence, local support, and in many cases a local reseller or system integrator as distribution channel; direct from US → Japan is not the market norm; (5) Pricing currency: EUR for Germany, JPY for Japan — billing in USD creates FX risk for the customer and signals lack of local commitment
Only language translation is required for B2B software international expansion
Enterprise software companies should use identical sales approaches in all markets
explanation: B2B software internationalization: the US SaaS model (self-serve, product-led growth, low-touch sales) is optimized for US market characteristics (comfortable with online purchasing, credit card billing, English as enterprise standard). European and Asian enterprise markets require different go-to-market approaches. Salesforce's Japan entry: Salesforce entered Japan through a partnership with SoftBank (a major Japanese telecom and technology conglomerate); SoftBank provided local sales force, relationships with Japanese enterprises, and credibility as a local partner. This accelerated Salesforce Japan's growth but required sharing economics. The GDPR data residency requirement: not just a legal constraint — it's a sales objection that can block deals. 'Where is my data stored?' is a security review question in every European enterprise deal; a company that can answer 'EU region data centers' removes the objection. The Germany-Japan difference: Germany and Japan are both high-UAI (Uncertainty Avoidance) cultures requiring formal processes, but their specific procurement norms differ — German IT procurement emphasizes security certification and data protection; Japanese IT procurement emphasizes relationship-based trust and established vendor credibility.

question: A global consumer brand (Nike) must decide whether to respond to a controversy in China: Chinese social media users are criticizing Nike for a statement about Xinjiang cotton sourcing that implied concern about forced labor. The Chinese government and state media are amplifying the criticism. Nike's options: (A) Maintain the statement (consistent global values); (B) Retract the statement (appease Chinese market); (C) Stay silent and wait. Nike's revenue from China is $2B+ (10%+ of total revenue). Evaluate the strategic and ethical dimensions of this decision.
options: Companies should always prioritize protecting local market revenue over consistent global values

This is a values consistency vs. market revenue trade-off with asymmetric global stakeholder implications: the stakeholder analysis: Chinese consumers and government: maintaining the statement risks Chinese government retaliation, consumer boycott, and retailer delisting in China ($2B revenue risk). Global consumers, NGOs, regulators (US, EU): retracting the statement to appease Chinese government signals that the brand's values are for sale; ESG investors will note the value capitulation; US legislators have scrutinized companies that comply with Chinese pressure; EU supply chain due diligence laws require statements on forced labor sourcing. Employees: global workforce that sees leadership capitulate to authoritarian pressure vs. Chinese employees who face retaliation if the company maintains political positions. The historical cases: H&M, Nike, Burberry, Adidas all faced Chinese boycotts over Xinjiang cotton statements; H&M faced more severe consequences (removed from Chinese e-commerce platforms); the brands that maintained their positions eventually saw Chinese market normalized (within 2 years) while the brands that fully capitulated faced ongoing escalation demands. The strategic lesson: Chinese market pressure is a test — companies that capitulate demonstrate they can be pressured, inviting future escalation; companies that maintain positions lose short-term revenue but preserve strategic integrity. The ethical dimension: if the forced labor concern is substantively true, capitulating to protect revenue is choosing market access over human rights — a choice with moral weight beyond the financial calculation
Companies should always prioritize the largest revenue market in any controversy
Ethics are irrelevant when large revenue sources are at stake
explanation: The Xinjiang cotton controversy (2021): Nike, H&M, Burberry, and others made statements about Xinjiang cotton sourcing; Chinese social media (with government amplification) organized boycotts. Nike's response was more nuanced than full retraction — neither fully capitulating nor fully escalating. H&M was removed from Chinese e-commerce platforms (Alibaba, JD.com) and faced a more severe response. The strategic analysis of option A (maintain position): Nike's global brand is worth >$30B; capitulating to Chinese government pressure for $2B in market revenue sets a precedent that the brand can be moved by market pressure — inviting similar pressure from other governments and on other issues. The financial calculation: losing $2B China revenue for 2 years (Chinese consumer memory is finite) costs ~$4B; retaining the global brand's credibility as a values-consistent brand protects the 90% of revenue outside China from similar pressure from other governments, and from ESG-sensitive institutional investors. Nike's actual behavior: Nike made no public retraction; the Chinese market controversy moderated over 12-18 months; Nike resumed meaningful growth in China thereafter. The lesson from multiple cases: short-term boycott pain without capitulation tends to end; capitulation invites escalating demands.
